data.title: NGL Energy: Beneficial 2026 Guidance And Undervalued, But With Low Margin Of Safety (Downgrade)
data.description:
NGL Energy Partners LP (NYSE:NGL) recently provided a beneficial 2026 Adjusted EBITDA of about $615-$625 million, which implies a valuation close to 7.3x 2026 Adjusted EBITDA. The company reported the acquisition of shares under its common unit repurchase program, and noted the simplification of its business mix and sale of assets to lower its total net debt. Considering the recent increase in the crude oil price, I redesigned my financial model, and obtained an implied valuation that is significantly higher than the price reported in my previous article. I do see risks from changes in the regulations about GHG Emissions and new regulatory rules imposed by the SEC. With all that being said, I do believe that NGL continues to be undervalued. When I first covered NGL, the stock traded at $3.32. Right now, NGL trades at about $4.5 per share.

Business Model

NGL Energy Partners offers a wide range of services including transportation, blending, and marketing of crude oil as well as water solutions.

The company operates in the Delaware Basin, the DJ Basin in Colorado, and the Eagle Ford Basin in Texas. In the last annual report, NGL reported 569 employees. The largest revenue comes from water transportation. The company appears to operate the largest network of large diameter produced water pipelines in the Northern Delaware Basin. In the last quarterly report, the company noted property and equipment of about $2.066 billion and book value close to $697.4 million. The current market capitalization is not far from $600 million. Given these figures, in my view, most investors would say that NGL appears quite undervalued.

Our core asset in the Water Solutions segment is our system located in the Northern Delaware Basin, where we own and operate the largest integrated network of large diameter produced water pipelines, recycling facilities and disposal wells. Source: 10-k

I recommend readers to have a look at my previous article about the company, where I offered a lot more information about the business model. We made beneficial stock returns thanks to NGL, and I believe that the company could distribute more stock returns in the near future. In this article, I am providing a new financial model, with more assumptions and a new price target.

In the most recent press release, NGL reported 2026 Adjusted EBITDA of about $615-$625 million. If we assume an enterprise value of $4.54 billion, the company appears to be trading at not far from 7.3x 2026 Adjusted EBITDA. The company does not look expensive right now. I took into account the guidance given by NGL to design my financial model.

We are guiding Fiscal 2026 full year consolidated Adjusted EBITDA to a range of $615 -$625 million which is an increase over Fiscal 2025 actuals adjusted for EBITDA associated with asset sales. Also, we are guiding to $45 million in maintenance and $60 million of growth capital expenditures for Fiscal 2026. Source: News Release

Strategic Actions To Accelerate Cash Flow Generation Will Most Likely Enhance The Demand For The Stock

In 2024 and 2025, NGL sold a significant number of assets in order to lower the total amount of debt as well as to invest in core areas in which the NGL has expertise. In this regard, in the last annual report, which was issued a few weeks ago, the management provided the following information. NGL appears to be focusing on offering stable cash flow generation, an improvement of the credit profile, and a simplification of the business mix. With this in mind, I would expect new transactions to accelerate free cash flow in 2025 and 2026. As soon as more investors understand the initiatives of NGL, in my opinion, the demand for the stock will most likely increase.

Over the past several years, we have undertaken a number of important strategic actions in an effort to capitalize on the Partnership’s core areas of competitive strength and focus on generating stable, growing and predictable cash flows, while improving our credit profile. We believe our actions have simplified our business mix and have allowed us to focus on what we believe are the core areas of our business and improved our overall financial position. Source: 10-k

The Common Unit Repurchase Program And Reduction In The Unit Could Enhance The Stock Price

Considering the valuation of the stock, NGL decided to acquire its own shares. In 2024, the company launched a share buyback program worth $50 million that, I believe, could accelerate the demand for the stock. In the year ended March 31, 2025, the company acquired 500,000 units for $2.1 million. It means that the company acquired shares at close to $4.2 per share. Right now, the stock does not trade far from the price mark at which the company bought shares.

On June 5, 2024, the board of directors of our GP authorized a common unit repurchase program, under which we may repurchase up to $50.0 million of our outstanding common units from time to time in the open market or in other privately negotiated transactions. This program does not have a fixed expiration date. The common unit repurchase program authorization does not obligate us to repurchase any dollar amount or number of common units. During the year ended March 31, 2025, we repurchased 500,000 units for an aggregate price of $2.1 million, including commissions. Source: 10-k

Regulations With Regard To GHG Emissions Could Deteriorate Future Net Income Growth

NGL and the oil and gas industry could be affected by changes in the regulation of GHG emissions. The Obama administration issued a number of rules that became effective in 2016. The Trump administration did change many of the rules that the Obama administration enacted.

Besides, I believe that in the coming years, the Trump administration will most likely change and relax certain GHG NSPS’s requirements. With that, I am not in a position to forecast when these changes will be effective, and whether they will enhance the valuation of the stock. In this regard, the annual report included the following commentary.

During the Obama Administration, the EPA finalized three rules that regulate GHG emissions from certain sources in the oil and natural gas industry, including New Source Performance Standards for the Oil and Natural Gas Sector, which became effective on August 2, 2016. During the Trump Administration, rulemaking was undertaken resulting in a substantial relaxation in the GHG NSPS’s requirements, including those relating to fugitive emissions, pneumatic pump standards, and closed vent system certification, among other things, which were finalized on August 13, 2020. Source: 10-k

There are also a number of new regulations issued by the SEC, which include a number of climate-related disclosures. NGL may have to invest a lot of money to understand and follow the new guidelines issued by the SEC. In addition, investors may not appreciate the information disclosed by NGL. As a result, the demand for the stock would most likely lower, which may lead to declines in the stock price. In this regard, NGL provided the following information in the last 10-k.

On March 6, 2024, the Securities and Exchange Commission (“SEC”) adopted a new set of rules that require a wide range of climate-related disclosures, including material climate-related risks, information on any climate-related targets or goals that are material to the registrant’s business, results of operations, or financial condition, Scope 1 and Scope 2 GHG emissions on a phased-in basis by certain larger registrants when those emissions are material and the filing of an attestation report covering the same, and disclosure of the financial statement effects of severe weather events and other natural conditions including costs and losses. Source: 10-k

Risks From Concentration Of Clients

The company reports that the number of clients does not seem that significant. 73% of the total amount of revenue from the Water Solutions segment came from ten clients. In the future, if the company loses one of these clients, the decline in total revenue could be significant. The crude oil logistics segment also seems to report a limited number of clients. Under the worst-case scenario, we could see a decline in total free cash flow, which may also lead to a significant decline in the stock price.

During the year ended March 31, 2025, 73% of the revenues of our Water Solutions segment were generated from our ten largest customers of the segment. Additionally, certain key customers of the Water Solutions segment contribute significantly to the cash flows and profitability of the Partnership. Any loss of those customers or their contracts could have an adverse impact on our financial results. Source: 10-k

During the year ended March 31, 2025, 79% of the revenues of our Crude Oil Logistics segment were generated from our ten largest customers of the segment. Source: 10-k

New Valuation Model

I increased my free cash flow expectations because of the recent increase in the oil price and the recent guidance delivered by NGL. My FCF figures include $477 million in 2025, $512 million in 2030, and $625 million in 2035. Let's note that my new numbers did not change a lot as compared to what I reported in my previous article.

I also used a conservative terminal multiple of 7x, WACC of 10%, and a share count of 132 million. The implied valuation stood at $8.5, which is higher than my target price in my previous report. In my previous article, my target price was close to $6.12 per share.

I do believe that there is upside potential in the stock price. However, considering the recent increase in the stock price, I no longer see such a large margin of safety to buy shares.

According to Seeking Alpha, the previous article provided stock returns of about 36%. I decided to close my position in the stock because I did identify other opportunities elsewhere.

I understand that investors out there maintain their position in the company because it is clearly undervalued. The company trades at 2x TTM cash flow. Peers trade at 5.58x TTM cash flow.

My Final Words

NGL Energy Partners recently provided a guidance that implies a valuation not far from 7.3x 2026 Adjusted EBITDA. Management also promised to undertake strategic actions to deliver stable free cash flow, as well as to simplify the company's business mix. In this regard, I believe that we can expect further sale of assets and net debt decreases. Given the recent increases in the price of oil, I redesigned my financial model, and increased my FCF forecasts, which implied a higher target price than that in the previous article. Having said that, and considering the recent increase in the stock price, I think that the margin of safety decreased. There is upside potential in the stock price, and the company is undervalued. However, I decided to close my position because I found other opportunities elsewhere. I understand that investors are not closing their position because the company continues to be cheap at 2x TTM cash flow.

data.title: VEA: A High-Conviction Case For Global Diversification Beyond U.S. Borders
data.description:
Intro

Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VEA) is one of the most widely held ETFs for developed market equity exposure outside the United States. With an ultra-low expense ratio of just 0.03% and over $224 billion in assets under management, it checks the boxes for cost-conscious, long-term investors. Yet despite its widespread use, VEA often finds itself on the defensive. Critics cite its historical underperformance relative to U.S. stocks, pointing to the S&P 500’s dominance over the past 10 to 15 years as evidence that international diversification “doesn’t work.”

But that framing is overly narrow, and frankly, misses the point of owning something like VEA.

Investing isn’t about loading up on yesterday’s winners. It’s about building durable, well-balanced portfolios that can survive different market environments and leadership rotations. VEA, by design, offers something U.S. stocks can’t: exposure to regions with different currencies, monetary policy regimes, sector weightings, and valuation profiles. And while the recent rearview mirror may show lagging returns, there are decades-long periods, including the early 2000s, when developed international stocks outperformed handily.

What’s more, VEA introduces sector diversification that is almost impossible to replicate through domestic U.S. holdings alone. It leans heavier into financials and industrials, with less exposure to U.S.-centric mega-cap tech names. This sector tilt adds behavioral diversification as well as different return paths, different reactions to interest rate changes, different exposure to global GDP.

Importantly, VEA isn’t just a placeholder for “non-U.S. stuff.” It’s a comprehensive, broad-market ETF with nearly 4,000 holdings. It avoids overlap with U.S. stocks entirely, making it ideal for portfolios that want clean, uncorrelated sleeves. And compared to its peers, funds like (IEFA), (ESGD), or (DBEF), it remains the lowest cost, highest scale, and arguably most trustworthy option, backed by Vanguard’s deep indexing experience.

In this article, we’ll unpack VEA’s portfolio role by examining key performance data, cyclical return behavior, sector diversification, top holdings, and how it stacks up against its international ETF competitors. We’ll also explore how tilting a portion of your developed markets exposure to funds like AVDV, the Avantis International Small Cap Value ETF, can amplify the benefits. Look to some of my articles pertaining for more details on this, though.

The argument here is not that VEA is poised to beat U.S. equities in the next year. It’s that over a full investing lifecycle, VEA plays a structurally different role, and that difference is what improves outcomes. If you believe that future leadership rotates, that rebalancing works, and that diversification means more than just owning more tickers, VEA is an asset that deserves a long-term seat at the table.

Performance Metrics Deep Dive

Let’s start with the raw numbers. From August 1990 to June 2025, Developed Markets (DM) produced a 5.57% CAGR. Meanwhile, U.S. Large Cap (US LC) delivered 10.51% CAGR. That’s a staggering difference over 35 years. But now look at the volatility numbers—DM carried higher volatility at 16.21% versus 14.79% for the U.S. Worse Sharpe. Worse Sortino. Bigger drawdowns. It's a tough look.

But zoom out: the 50/50 blend of DM and US LC returns 8.12% CAGR with a volatility profile similar to U.S. stocks. It even improves on downside risk, slightly lowering drawdown volatility. The Sharpe and Sortino ratios rise to 0.37 and 0.33, respectively, clearly better than DM alone. So while DM has been weaker, it still plays a useful role in combination. This is portfolio math 101: combining imperfectly correlated assets smooths outcomes. Blended portfolios aren't about picking winners, they're about creating structures that maximize stability and increase compoundability.

Long-Term Growth Path

The portfolio value chart visualizes the compounding in action. U.S. stocks pulled far ahead, no question. But the 50/50 DM/US LC portfolio compounded to a middle-ground path well above DM alone. That gap widens most notably post-2010 when U.S. tech dominance kicked in. But during earlier periods, such as the 1990s and early 2000s, Developed Markets kept pace and even outperformed for stretches.

Blending DM and U.S. equities helps to capture this broader opportunity set. Periodic rebalancing between the two allows investors to systematically buy low and sell high as leadership rotates. These are not theoretical gains, they’re real contributors to long-term wealth that only appear when you zoom out to multi-decade holding periods.

Annual Returns

The annual returns bar chart further illustrates how regional leadership shifts year-to-year. In years like 1993, 1994, 2004, 2005, and especially 2009, Developed Markets beat U.S. equities. These differences don’t just make for talking points, they create opportunities. Systematic rebalancing from relative outperformers to laggards takes advantage of mean reversion over time, enhancing returns at the portfolio level.

This return rotation is also crucial from a risk management standpoint. By holding VEA, you introduce non-U.S. exposure that responds differently to macroeconomic trends, interest rate cycles, and geopolitical dynamics. It’s not that VEA will outperform every year. In fact, it probably won’t. But its mere difference in behavior supports better diversification outcomes.

Five-Year CAGR Windows

One of the best ways to visualize cyclical leadership is to observe rolling five-year return windows. While U.S. equities tend to dominate the average, there are clear periods when Developed Markets shine. Between 2003 and 2008, and again around 2017, the red line (DM) rises above the blue line (US LC), showing periods of sustained outperformance. This is not noise, it’s rotation.

What makes these cycles powerful is their persistence. When DM outperforms, it often does so for multiple years, not just isolated events. Owning VEA gives you a claim on those stretches. More importantly, the 50/50 blend’s green line smooths the experience, reducing the chance of behavioral missteps from investors chasing performance at exactly the wrong time.

Distribution of Returns

Analyzing the return distribution adds another layer of insight. U.S. Large Cap returns are tightly clustered around moderate gains but with longer positive tails. DM returns are more dispersed, with more modest outcomes and occasional sharp drawdowns. When these two are combined into a blended portfolio, the distribution becomes taller, narrower, and more stable.

This matters for both retirees and accumulators. For retirees drawing from their portfolio, fewer extreme months reduce the likelihood of sequence risk—that is, poor returns early in retirement that jeopardize sustainability. For younger investors, smoother monthly performance builds confidence, increasing the likelihood they’ll stay invested through volatility.

Drawdowns and Recovery

The drawdown chart reveals that DM investors endured a 57.37% peak-to-trough decline, which took over 5 years to fully recover. U.S. equities experienced a slightly shallower 50.97% drawdown but bounced back in just under 3.5 years. The 50/50 blend split the difference, a slightly smaller drawdown and faster recovery.

This might sound marginal, but during real-time stress events, even small improvements in drawdown duration and depth can make the difference between staying the course and panic-selling. VEA alone is hard to hold during these events. But as part of a diversified blend, it reduces overall fragility.

Correlations and Complementarity

Correlation between U.S. and Developed Markets equity is 0.84. This is not independent, but low enough to offer diversification. Compare that to U.S. Large and Mid Cap stocks, which often exhibit correlations over 0.95. When constructing portfolios, this marginal difference enables more effective rebalancing and dampens volatility clusters.

What makes VEA unique is not just that it's international, it’s that it offers exposure to different policy regimes, economic sensitivities, and sectoral weightings. In short, it introduces return paths you can't get from U.S. equities alone. That’s real diversification, not just holding more stocks, but holding different drivers of risk and return.

Peer Comparison

One of VEA’s most compelling advantages is its simplicity and cost structure. At just 0.03% in expenses, it is among the lowest-cost international ETFs available. This makes it ideal for long-term investors seeking efficiency. In fact, when stacked up against other international equity funds like (IEFA) (0.07%), (ESGD) (0.21%), (DBEF) (0.35%), (GSIE) (0.25%), and (RODM) (0.29%), VEA clearly leads on pricing.

But the edge doesn’t stop at cost. VEA also leads in scale. With over $224 billion in assets under management (AUM), it is by far the largest developed market ETF, eclipsing the next largest, IEFA, at $138 billion. That scale matters. Higher AUM often correlates with tighter bid-ask spreads, better tax efficiency, and stronger liquidity—especially during periods of stress or rebalancing. For institutional or high-volume traders, that reliability is a hidden but vital feature.

Another underrated aspect of VEA is its brand. Vanguard has a long-standing reputation for investor-first practices, low costs, and rigorous index construction. In an environment where fund families continue to launch thematic or narrow-focus products with unclear mandates, VEA’s broad, consistent, and rules-based approach is both refreshing and valuable. Vanguard’s stewardship, scale, and trust give long-term allocators the confidence to stick with it through periods of underperformance.

Diversification and Structure

Beyond price and reputation, VEA is structurally sound in ways that complement U.S.-centric portfolios. The sector breakdown adds meaningful diversification. Unlike the S&P 500, which is heavily tilted toward technology and communications, VEA’s largest sector weightings are in Financials (22.91%) and Industrials (18.34%), followed by Technology (10.27%), Health Care (10.04%), and Consumer Cyclical (9.36%).

This makes VEA less reliant on mega-cap tech and more exposed to economic cyclicals, global banks, industrial machinery, and pharmaceuticals. These are sectors that tend to do well during periods of global economic growth or reflation, providing valuable rotation potential when U.S. growth stocks stumble. That’s not just sector diversification; it’s behavioral diversification too. Different sectors react to different shocks. Holding them together smooths the overall ride.

The diversification is also geographic and monetary. With no U.S. exposure, VEA avoids duplication. Its 3,800+ holdings span Europe, Japan, Australia, and Canada, exposing investors to varied fiscal and monetary regimes, demographics, and currencies. This makes VEA one of the most complete international funds on the market.

Top Holdings Insight

VEA’s top 10 holdings as of April 2025 are led by SAP SE (SAP), Nestle SA ( OTCPK:NSRGY) , ASML (ASML) , Novartis (NVS) , and Toyota (OTCPK:TOYOF). These firms operate in industries such as enterprise software, semiconductors, consumer staples, and pharmaceuticals. Importantly, these companies represent real economic activity in Europe and Asia, uncorrelated with U.S. tech earnings cycles.

Additionally, none of the top 10 exceed a 1.32% portfolio weight. This makes the fund relatively unconcentrated at the top. Compare that to U.S. ETFs, where Apple (AAPL) and Microsoft (MSFT) can often account for 7–10% combined. This structural dispersion reduces single-stock risk and adds to portfolio resilience.

In total, VEA holds 3,886 stocks, offering extremely broad exposure without overconcentration in any one firm or sector. For investors who want to avoid top-heavy portfolios while still capturing international growth, VEA’s structure is hard to beat.

Conclusion

VEA isn’t a home run hitter. It’s not the fastest horse in the race, and it likely won’t top any return leaderboard over the next 12 months. But that doesn’t mean it doesn’t matter. In fact, the more seriously you take portfolio construction, the more you realize that assets like VEA are exactly what keep portfolios intact, not because they dominate, but because they do something even more valuable: they diversify what matters.

Developed Markets have underperformed U.S. equities for much of the past decade, but that underperformance has not been linear, and it won’t last forever. Asset class leadership rotates. Valuations mean-revert. Global economic cycles change. And when those shifts come, the benefits of holding a truly global equity sleeve are amplified, not just through performance, but through improved rebalancing opportunities, smoother multi-asset dynamics, and reduced sequence risk.

VEA is also unmatched in structural efficiency. It offers clean, complete exposure to non-U.S. developed economies at a fraction of the cost of its peers. It avoids overlap with U.S. stocks. It’s not concentrated in a handful of mega-cap names. It owns nearly 4,000 stocks across dozens of countries and sectors, and does it all for three basis points. You can’t build that on your own.

Beyond just data, there’s also a behavioral layer to this argument. A portfolio that contains assets that behave differently, especially ones like VEA that zig when others zag, is a portfolio that helps investors stick to the plan. And staying invested matters more than squeezing out marginal performance. VEA reduces fragility, increases discipline, and gives investors the emotional bandwidth to withstand future uncertainty.

For those seeking to go further, tilting VEA’s exposure with a value-focused ETF like AVDV adds additional upside potential. AVDV complements VEA’s broad market beta with proven factor exposure to international small-cap value stocks, an equity sleeve that has historically delivered stronger risk-adjusted returns while remaining uncorrelated to U.S. large-cap growth.

In total, VEA may never be the most exciting fund in your portfolio. But over 30 years, it may be one of the most important. Not because it outperforms every year, but because it plays a role no other asset class can replicate: a differentiated, diversified, low-cost counterbalance to the U.S. equity machine. And when the cycle turns, as it always does, those who made space for VEA will be glad they did.

Attribution

All charts and performance data sourced from factorlytics.io as of June 2025. Holdings, sector data, and peer ETF comparisons retrieved from Vanguard and Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Dino Polska: Mixed Q1 Results, But Long Term Growth Is On Track
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Dino Polska (OTCPK:DNOPY) was on March 16. In that article, I gave the company a buy rating. Since then, it has returned 14.42% compared to the S&P 500's 6.13%. Despite the positive performance difference, Dino is down nearly 10% from its all-time highs. Additionally, it released its Q1 results in the meantime, which were apparently weaker than expected — but there's an explanation.

I always say that Dino is somewhat opaque when it comes to their communications. They don’t hold earnings conference calls, so I’ll rely on the presentation and the report they shared to write this article.

Thesis Review

This is a fragment from my previous article that I believe summarizes the company very well.

Let's briefly recall the thesis for Dino Polska. Dino is a Polish supermarket chain with a strong presence in the western part of the country. It's characterized by having a founder-led management, high vertical integration, ownership of its store locations and the land it operates on, as well as its own meat processing plants and distribution centers.

The company's main competitive advantage lies in its "local monopolies" in rural areas of the country. By focusing mainly on towns with no more than 5,000 inhabitants and maintaining a high level of agility in building stores (the company owns the firm that constructs its supermarkets), Dino makes it unprofitable for other competitors to enter these market niches.

If we recall, Dino's long-term bullish thesis is based on its ability to continue expanding the number of stores in Poland (currently 2,688) while maintaining organic growth above inflation. We will see how well this combination holds up, but it should drive sales growth in the mid-single-digit range. With good capital allocation, bottom-line growth should be in the high-double-digit range. Let's review its figures.

Earnings Review

Here we have several highlights from the quarter presented by the company. Let’s take a closer look at a few of them.

The first thing I would highlight, one of the pillars of the investment thesis, is that the pace of new store openings has reaccelerated (58 vs. 32 in Q1 2024). This is good news, especially considering Dino’s still significant total addressable market, which is estimated at at least 7,000 stores within Poland. This is undoubtedly one of the company’s key growth drivers and the main reason behind its capital reinvestment (more on this later).

My estimates for this year are that Dino will end with around 3,000 owned stores, which would represent a year-over-year growth rate of about 12%, or roughly 320 new stores (50 more than last year). If they maintain this accelerated pace of openings, it seems plausible.

Other highlights related to real estate and environmental measures. This continued progress is positive, as it makes Dino’s stores energy self-sufficient, which will ultimately improve margins through cost savings.

In the first quarter of 2025, a total of 2,569 Dino stores were equipped with their own photovoltaic installations, representing an increase of 93 stores compared to the previous quarter. The total capacity of the solar panels installed in these stores reached 103 MW, marking a 17% year-over-year growth. During this period, Dino stores generated 15.2 GWh of electricity from solar energy, compared to 10.4 GWh generated in the same quarter of 2024.

Without a doubt, the most concerning aspect in light of inflation was the company’s like-for-like (LFL) growth of just 0.5% compared to the same quarter last year. It’s very unusual for Dino to grow below the inflation rate (which was 6.1% in this case), but there is context behind it.

According to the Q1 report:

LFL sales growth in Q1 2025 was 0.5%, following 11.9% growth in the corresponding period of 2024. The drivers contributing to the relatively low LFL sales growth rate in Q1 2025 were as follows: i) the shift in the timing of the Easter Holiday from Q1 2024 to Q2 2025 (which in turn should favorably affect the LFL sales growth rate in Q2 of this year) and ii) two fewer trading days in Q1 2025 versus Q1 2024.

In the first quarter of 2025, costs as a percentage of sales remained relatively stable or showed slight improvements. The cost of sales accounted for 75.7% of revenue, slightly below the 76.9% recorded in Q1 2024, indicating a modest improvement in gross margin. However, selling and marketing expenses increased as a proportion of revenue, driven by the expansion of the store network and comparable sales growth. General and administrative expenses also rose, though to a lesser extent. Overall, while absolute costs increased, the cost of sales as a percentage of revenue showed a slight efficiency gain.

While the EBITDA margin has slightly declined, it’s due to higher marketing expenses tied to the accelerated pace of new store openings — which makes sense. The clear positive, however, is the increase in gross margin, a sign that the economy remains focused on cost savings and that Dino’s scale allows it to negotiate better prices with suppliers. This represents an operational improvement, whereas the rise in OPEX is a self-inflicted effect of the current expansion phase. I remain confident.

It’s clearly visible in the image.

As expected, the accelerated pace of store openings has significantly increased CAPEX (from 330M to 516M). Far from being a cause for concern, it’s something I’m actually pleased to see. This is what I wrote in my previous article:

If we look at its cash generation, we can see that Dino is in positive FCF territory for the second consecutive year.

After reinvesting in the business through CAPEX (1.6B PLN), the 951M PLN generated in FCF has primarily been used to repay debt. My hypothesis is that in the coming years, Dino will start distributing dividends or repurchasing shares.

Until 2022, FCF was negative because Dino reinvested everything into opening new stores. We have now reached a point where the company generates so much cash that it will have to start returning it to investors.

But considering that Dino has ROICs of around 23%, it’s preferable that the company continues reinvesting in the business (as long as management expects similar returns) rather than distributing capital to shareholders. Given the TAM still ahead, I’d rather see them keep opening stores and gaining scale. The time to become a cash cow and start paying dividends and doing buybacks will come later.

On the other hand, debt continues to decline and is now down to just 0.31x.

Valuation

On the valuation side, nothing has changed—except that with the recent increase in the share price, the expected returns have gone from 12.3% back in March to around 11% currently. Just as a reminder, I’m projecting an annual store opening rate of 10–12%, 5% LFL growth, and an EBITDA margin of 8.5% by 2029. If we assign a 15x multiple, the resulting market cap would be 84,000 million PLN versus the current 50,000 million.

For all these reasons, I’m maintaining my buy rating, despite the stock price losing some momentum in the short term.

Risks

The risks the company faces remain unchanged.

Taking into account that it operates in a market niche with little technological disruption, offering a significant value proposition for the customer, boasting strong competitive advantages, and being capable of passing cost inflation increases onto higher prices, perhaps the company's greatest risk lies in key personnel.

As we have already discussed, Dino's CEO is its founder and has been responsible for evolving the company to its current state. While he is still 50 years old and heavily involved in the company's day-to-day operations, a change in leadership could entail a shift in business strategy and its consequent performance. Although nothing has been mentioned regarding this matter and he still has many years until retirement, I consider this risk to be the greatest for Dino."

However, the second biggest risk facing this type of sector is competition. It's important to keep in mind that supermarkets don't create switching costs or extreme brand loyalty. This is why it tends to be a sector with very fierce competition. While Dino tends to outperform its rivals, the differences often end up being determined by management. We'll have to monitor industry trends to see if Dino is being overtaken by the competition.

Conclusion

And that’s all the information from the Q1 results the company has presented. One might consider these results somewhat mixed, but the drop in sales and margin is understandable and has clear reasons behind it. On the other hand, the increase in reinvestment is a positive development, and everything seems to indicate that Dino is on track to meet my store opening estimates.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
